title: Lesson 6 Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Welcome to lesson six, Luis — the last lesson in module two. We'll cover dining-table vocabulary, traditional Maltese food, the question words like who and where, and one specific present-tense pattern: verbs that contain the letter għ.
First, the dining table. Plate is
Many of these have plural forms that look quite different from the singular — knife is sikkina but knives is
fork is furketta but forks is
These are common irregular plurals you'll learn naturally with use.
Traditional food. Some absolute classics. Snails:
And the famous flaky savoury pastry:
Twelve traditional dishes in total — the section reads more like a menu than a vocab list.
Now Wh-questions. The eight basic question words. Who is

These slot in at the start of a question, as they do in English. There's a short story in the section about Mark and Maria playing football, and questions about who, when, where, and why — practising all the question words at once.

Lastly, the trickiest grammar of the lesson: present-tense verbs that contain the letter għ. The letter is silent or stretched, but it changes how the verb is written, and Maltese learners follow two model verbs — Rifes for verbs that start with għ, and seRaq for verbs where għ sits in the middle.
It follows the Rifes model. I do is
Notice how the prefix slips before the għ. Now take 'to play',
It follows the seRaq model — għ in the middle. I play is
Don't worry if this feels dense — the conjugation tables in the section show every form, and the exercises drill it gently.
That's it. Six lessons, two modules. From here you can revisit any lesson, drill any section, or just listen to these overviews while you commute. The work compounds. Saħħa, Luis. Prosit.
